mainEntity.name: What is the minimum camera I need for amateur sport?

acceptedAnswer.text: Any modern body with continuous autofocus and a burst rate of at least 6fps will get you started. A used Canon R10, Nikon Z50 II, or Sony α6400 paired with a 70-300mm zoom will handle most amateur sport. Spend on the lens before the body — reach matters more than resolution.

mainEntity.name: Do I need a monster telephoto lens?

acceptedAnswer.text: For full-size football, rugby, or cricket pitches you'll want at least 300mm at the long end. A 100-400mm zoom is the sport sweet spot — versatile and affordable second-hand. For swimming, basketball, gymnastics or indoor cricket, a 70-200mm is plenty. Do not buy 600mm for under-12s — you'll be too far away to read body language.

mainEntity.name: What ISO is too high for sport photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: Modern bodies handle ISO 3200 well and ISO 6400 usably. Take whatever ISO you need to keep your shutter speed up — grain is fixable in editing, motion blur is not. Pre-2018 bodies typically struggle above ISO 1600; test your specific body on a static subject before relying on it for action.

mainEntity.name: Should I shoot in shutter priority or full manual mode?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both work. Shutter priority is the easier start — set your shutter speed and the camera handles the rest. Full manual gives more control when light changes during the match. Most parents starting out should use shutter priority for their first few sessions; switch to manual when you understand how the camera is making its aperture and ISO choices.

mainEntity.name: Can I photograph my child's school sports day?

acceptedAnswer.text: Photographing your own child is fine; sharing the photos publicly is the question. Most schools allow personal photography on the understanding that images aren't shared on social media without consent from other parents whose children appear. Check the school's photography policy before publishing — it almost always covers this.

mainEntity.name: How do I photograph indoor sports without using flash?

acceptedAnswer.text: Indoor sport at most levels prohibits flash. The answer is fast glass plus high ISO — a 70-200mm f/2.8 at ISO 3200-6400 will get sharp images of basketball, netball, gymnastics, indoor cricket. Slow zooms (f/4+) cannot keep up with the required shutter speeds. The f/2.8 zoom is the most useful single piece of kit for indoor sport.

mainEntity.name: Should I edit sport photos differently from other genres?

acceptedAnswer.text: The workflow is the same: import, cull, set intent, global adjustments, tonal refinement, local adjustments, output. Sport-specific points: cull more aggressively (5-10% keeper rate is normal), keep edits relatively clean rather than heavily stylised, and crop in-post to tighten composition when in-camera framing was loose.

mainEntity.name: Is panning worth the miss rate?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, used as a deliberate creative choice for specific moments rather than a default. A panned shot at 1/60-1/125s has perhaps a 1 in 20 sharp rate, but the frame that works communicates speed better than any freeze. Mix frozen action with panned moments for a stronger gallery; the miss rate drops as your technique improves.
